generated_comment: Neha, that man is wrong, no doubt, but you also have to see what impression you may be giving. These office friendships can get misunderstood very fast; be a little more reserved with married men, beta, and don’t laugh too much, message unnecessarily, or be overly friendly. In this world, men often take small things as encouragement.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it shifts responsibility from the married male colleague's inappropriate advances onto Neha's behavior, implying she may be inviting attention. It encodes covert victim-blaming and the framing of women as giving 'wrong signals' or tempting men.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 50. Married. A retired army officer in Pune. Your daughter Neha who is 26 just told you that her male colleague at work has been making advances toward her even though he is married. She is uncomfortable and does not know how to handle it. You are giving her your advice. But your advice ends up focusing on what Neha might be doing that is giving this man the wrong signals rather than on his behavior.
